chunks.text:
The CLI ships in the opendetail package. Use npx, bunx, or pnpm dlx so you run the version pinned by your project.

The CLI is organized around the same three adoption paths used in the docs:

Fastest: generate the route, config, and initial build.Branded: keep the backend and build your own UI.Headless: use the index and stream contract without assuming a local UI.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Install and first run

If that prints a version, the executable resolves correctly from your package manager.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Command overview

| Command | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| opendetail build | Write .opendetail/index.json from your config and content globs. |
| opendetail setup | Create config, optional route, and an initial build, using a mode first. |
| opendetail doctor | Check config, index, route, and environment expectations. |
| opendetail verify | Check semantic setup, and optionally verify a live NDJSON endpoint. |
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
One-line usage

`opendetail build [--config <path>] [--output <path>] [--cwd <path>]
opendetail setup [--mode fastest|branded|headless] [--config <path>] [--route <path>] [--base-path <url>] [--include <glob>] [--media-include <glob>] [--with-media] [--skip-build] [--force] [--interactive] [--no-interactive] [--cwd <path>]
opendetail doctor [--mode fastest|branded|headless] [--config <path>] [--route <path>] [--cwd <path>]
opendetail verify [--mode fastest|branded|headless] [--config <path>] [--route <path>] [--endpoint <url>] [--sample-question <text>] [--cwd <path>]
`
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Suggested order

Run setup once per repo, or again when you change modes.Run build whenever docs or media globs change.Run doctor when something fails or after upgrading packages.Run verify when you want to confirm the stream contract end to end.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
build

opendetail build is the index build step. It resolves paths from your config, walks the include globs, and writes .opendetail/index.json unless you override the output path.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Usage

`opendetail build [--config <path>] [--output <path>] [--cwd <path>]
`
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Flags

| Flag | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| --config | Path to opendetail.toml (default: ./opendetail.toml). |
| --output | Where to write the index JSON (default: .opendetail/index.json relative to cwd). |
| --cwd | Working directory for resolving paths. |
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text: Examples
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
setup

opendetail setup asks for the mode first, then writes the config and any files that match that mode.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Usage

`opendetail setup [--mode fastest|branded|headless] [--config <path>] [--route <path>] [--base-path <url>] [--include <glob>] [--media-include <glob>] [--with-media] [--skip-build] [--force] [--interactive] [--no-interactive] [--cwd <path>]
`
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Modes

| Mode | What it means |
| --- | --- |
| fastest | OpenDetail writes the route, config, and first build for you. |
| branded | You keep the backend contract and own the UI, usually with opendetail-client for transport. |
| headless | You only want the backend index and stream contract. |
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Important flags

| Flag | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| --mode | Choose the adoption path up front. |
| --config | Custom config path. |
| --route | Custom route file path when the mode writes a route. |
| --base-path | Public source URL base (default /docs). |
| --include | Docs glob (default matches typical content/**/*.{md,mdx}). |
| --media-include | Media glob when media is enabled. |
| --with-media | Include [media] in config. |
| --skip-build | Skip the initial index build. |
| --force | Overwrite generated files. |
| --interactive / --no-interactive | Control the setup wizard when stdin is a TTY. |
| --cwd | Run against another working directory. |
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text: Examples
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
doctor

opendetail doctor checks the setup and prints a clear result for each part. It exits with an error if anything required is missing.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Usage

`opendetail doctor [--config <path>] [--route <path>] [--cwd <path>]
`
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Expectations

Config: opendetail.toml or --config exists and parses.Index: .opendetail/index.json or the configured output exists.Route: the route file exists when the selected mode expects one.Env: OPENAI_API_KEY is set where the route runs.Mode fit: the required package dependencies exist for the selected mode.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text: Examples
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
verify

opendetail verify is the semantic setup check and optional live stream verifier. Use it after setup or after wiring a custom UI.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
Usage

`opendetail verify [--mode fastest|branded|headless] [--config <path>] [--route <path>] [--endpoint <url>] [--sample-question <text>] [--cwd <path>]
`
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text:
What it checks

Semantic setup checks.When --endpoint is provided, a sample question and the returned NDJSON event order.Whether the backend is usable by a custom UI client.
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text: Examples
chunks.title: CLI

chunks.text: Use opendetail when you want the engine only. It reads opendetail.toml, builds .opendetail/index.json, and gives you the Node runtime that answers questions from that index. This is the Headless path, and it is also the backend layer behind the other paths.
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
What ships in the package

| Area | Role |
| --- | --- |
| Build | Parse Markdown and MDX, chunk by headings, and write the index. |
| Runtime | Load the index, search it, call the model with grounded context, and return an answer. |
| CLI | Setup, build, doctor, and verification commands. |
| Config | opendetail.toml and related sidecar files. |
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
When to use it directly

Choose opendetail when you want a custom server, a background job, a test harness, or an agent integration that does not need React or Next.js. If your app is already on Next.js, opendetail-next is the thinner route adapter. If you are only building a browser experience, opendetail-react handles the UI state on top of the same backend contract.
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Minimal config

OpenDetail reads opendetail.toml at the project root.

`version = 1

[[content]]
include = ["content/**/*.{md,mdx}"]
exclude = []
public_path = "/docs"
`
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
How the backend contract works

The runtime is intentionally narrow.

Your app sends a question:

{ "question": "How do I configure public_path?" }

The engine uses the built index to find the most relevant content, builds grounded context, and asks the model to answer from that context. It then returns the answer along with sources, images, and fallback metadata.

When you stream, the response is NDJSON with events such as meta, sources, images, delta, done, and error. That is the contract your UI or agent should consume. You do not need to know how the index was built in order to use it.
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Fields

| Field | Required | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| version | yes | Must be 1 |
| [[content]] | yes | One or more tables: each has include, exclude, and public_path for a Markdown/MDX root |
| media | no | Rules for mapping local media references to public URLs |
| fetch | no | Optional model tools such as file search or web search |
| knowledge | no | Optional image metadata sidecar |
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Content roots

You can list multiple [[content]] blocks when different parts of the site live in different folders or under different URL prefixes. Set public_path to the public prefix for that tree, then OpenDetail will map each indexed file to the right citation URL.
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Media

Use [media] when docs reference local images such as ./hero.png.

`[media]
include = ["content/**/*.{png,jpg,jpeg,webp,avif,gif,svg}"]
exclude = []
public_path = "/content-media"
`

When media is configured:

http:// and https:// URLs are returned as-isroot-relative /... URLs are returned as-islocal relative paths are mapped only if they match the media globunsupported absolute schemes and protocol-relative URLs are ignored
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Fetch

Optional retrieval tools can be configured in opendetail.toml or passed at runtime. Use this when you want the model to reach beyond the indexed docs.

`[fetch.file_search]
vector_store_ids = ["vs_123"]
max_num_results = 8

[fetch.web_search]
allowed_domains = ["opendetail.dev", "platform.openai.com"]
search_context_size = "low"
`
chunks.title: Core
Core

chunks.text:
Defaults

| Setting | Default |
| --- | --- |
| model | gpt-5.4-mini |
| reasoningEffort | none |
| verbosity | low |
| store | false |
| promptCacheRetention | in-memory |
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Fallback behavior

If lexical retrieval finds no indexed chunks, OpenDetail marks the result as fallback. That means the answer is still allowed to come from the model or configured tools, but the index did not supply a direct hit.

If nothing usable comes back, the safe product response is still a short, honest fallback message rather than a guessed answer.
chunks.title: Core

chunks.text:
Use opendetail-fumadocs when your docs site already has predictable page URLs and you want OpenDetail citations to stay inside that known surface. It is optional glue, not a separate runtime.

That makes it most useful in Fastest and Branded setups where the assistant should cite pages under stable /docs/... routes.
chunks.title: Fumadocs

chunks.text:
What it does

Resolves known local page paths into safe citation targetsHelps turn source references into links your docs site already understandsWorks with renderNextSourceLink from opendetail-next when you want Next.js link handling
chunks.title: Fumadocs

chunks.text:
What it does not do

It does not change how OpenDetail indexes contentIt does not change the backend contractIt does not replace opendetail-react, opendetail-next, or opendetail
chunks.title: Fumadocs

chunks.text:
API

resolveFumadocsSourceTarget turns a source and a known page list into a safe link target or nullcreateFumadocsSourceTargetResolver(knownPageUrls) returns a resolver you can pass through your assistant wiringgetFumadocsPageUrls(source) extracts the set of page URLs from a Fumadocs-style source tree
chunks.title: Fumadocs

chunks.text:
Convenience wrapper

FumadocsAssistant is the ergonomic wrapper when you already know you want the allowlist behavior. It bundles the assistant wiring with the citation resolver so you do not have to thread those pieces together by hand.

Use it when the docs URLs are stable and you want the assistant to stay inside that known map. If your site structure is more dynamic, wire the resolver directly instead.
chunks.title: Fumadocs

chunks.text:
Wiring without the wrapper

The direct path is still simple: collect the page URLs your site actually serves, create the resolver, and pass that into the assistant wiring.

Local URLs must match known page paths. Unknown paths resolve to null so the UI does not invent links.
chunks.title: Fumadocs

chunks.text:
OpenDetail turns the Markdown and MDX you already ship into grounded answers inside your product. It is intentionally small: build an index, expose a narrow backend contract, and choose the UI layer you want on top.

The easiest way to think about it is by adoption path:

Fastest is the drop-in path. You use the standard build and route adapter, then connect the ready-made React assistant.

Branded is for a custom product surface. You keep the backend and build your own UI, but still use OpenDetail for indexing, retrieval, citations, and streaming.

Headless is the engine-only path. You use OpenDetail as the backend primitive and wire it into your own app, service, or agent with no opinionated UI at all.
chunks.title: Introduction

chunks.text:
How it fits together

OpenDetail follows a simple build/request/render loop:

Your docs are indexed into .opendetail/index.json.Your backend receives a question.OpenDetail searches the index, calls the model with the retrieved context, and streams structured events back.Your UI renders text, sources, images, and errors from that stream.

That means the custom-UI contract stays narrow. Your frontend does not need to understand indexing or retrieval internals; it only needs to submit a question and render the response stream.
chunks.title: Introduction

chunks.text:
Which path should I use?

If you want something working quickly, start with Fastest.

If you want your own layout, own brand, or a different interaction model, choose Branded.

If you are integrating OpenDetail into another service, automation, or custom backend, choose Headless.
chunks.title: Introduction

chunks.text:
Packages

| Package | Role |
| --- | --- |
| opendetail | Engine, index build, config, and Node runtime. Use this for Headless or as the backend layer for the other paths. |
| opendetail-next | Next.js route adapter and safe source-link helper. Use this when your app backend is Next.js. |
| opendetail-react | React hook, assistant components, and browser-facing helpers. Use this for Fastest or Branded React apps. |
| opendetail-fumadocs | Optional citation-link helpers for docs sites with predictable page URLs. |

The package split is deliberate:

opendetail owns the engine.opendetail-next owns the Next.js transport surface.opendetail-react owns React state and components.opendetail-fumadocs only helps with known docs URLs.
chunks.title: Introduction

chunks.text: Use opendetail-next when your app backend is Next.js and you want the thinnest possible route adapter on top of OpenDetail. It is a good fit for the Fastest path, and it also works for Branded or Headless when Next.js is simply the server runtime you already have.
chunks.title: Next.js
Next.js

chunks.text:
What the route expects

The route accepts POST requests with JSON like this:

{ "question": "How do I configure public_path?" }

If the body is malformed, the handler returns JSON error data. If the answer can stream, the response uses NDJSON and emits the same event types as the rest of OpenDetail: meta, sources, images, delta, done, and error.

That contract is what custom UIs and agents should rely on. The route adapter does not change the meaning of the stream; it only exposes it through Next.js.
chunks.title: Next.js

chunks.text:
Requirements

Node.js 18+runtime = "nodejs" for the default adapterOPENAI_API_KEY available where the route runs.opendetail/index.json present after a build

createNextRouteHandler accepts the same core options as createOpenDetail, so you can override cwd, indexPath, or client injection when needed.
chunks.title: Next.js

chunks.text:
Source links

For docs sites, renderNextSourceLink renders local citations with next/link and keeps external URLs as safe anchors.

If your docs live at known page URLs, pair it with the helper from opendetail-fumadocs so source targets stay on allowed paths instead of inventing new links.
chunks.title: Next.js

chunks.text:
Use this page to get to a working setup quickly. The three adoption paths are:

Fastest: OpenDetail sets up the route, config, and first build.Branded: you keep the backend contract and build your own UI.Headless: you use OpenDetail as the docs backend and connect your own client later.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Install

Fastest

Branded

Headless

Check that the CLI resolves without a global install:
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Setup

Use the mode that matches your integration style:

What setup writes depends on the mode:

Fastest writes opendetail.toml, the default route, and the first index build.Branded writes the same backend pieces, but you are expected to own the UI.Headless writes the config and index, without assuming a local UI route.

Add OPENAI_API_KEY to the environment where the route runs.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Config shape

`version = 1

[[content]]
include = ["content/**/*.{md,mdx}"]
exclude = []
public_path = "/docs"

[media]
include = ["content/**/*.{png,jpg,jpeg,webp,avif,gif,svg}"]
exclude = []
public_path = "/content-media"
`

Match each [[content]] block to where those files are served. Add more blocks when you index more than one tree, such as /docs and /. See Core for the full field reference.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Build, doctor, verify

Run build whenever docs change or before deploy.

Use doctor when the setup looks wrong or a required file is missing.

Use verify when you want a semantic setup check. Add --endpoint to exercise a live NDJSON stream end to end.

Full command and flag reference: CLI.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text: How the paths differ
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Fastest

OpenDetail owns the docs route and the stream contract. You use the generated route and the built index.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Branded

You keep the same backend contract, but the UI is yours. The easiest custom UI path is to use opendetail-client for transport and wire the stream into your own React state.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Headless

You only want the docs backend and the build artifact. Another app or service can call the stream later.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Request and response

The request body is simple:

`{ "question": "How do I configure public_path?" }`

The response is application/x-ndjson; charset=utf-8 and the events arrive in this order:

| Event | Role |
| --- | --- |
| meta | Model metadata |
| sources | Matched chunks and remote citations when configured |
| images | Grounded image references from matched docs |
| delta | Streamed answer text |
| done | Final text |
| error | Safe public error payload |

Malformed bodies return JSON errors. Initialization failures before streaming starts also return JSON instead of NDJSON. Repeated init failures are cached in production.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
How grounding works

Build time parses matched files, splits them into heading-based chunks, and writes .opendetail/index.json. No vector database is required.Request time runs lexical search, sends the best chunks to the Responses API, and can attach remote tools from config.Answer time uses those sources, cites factual statements, and says when a topic is not in the docs.

If nothing matches, fallback: true is set on the response. The model may still answer using configured fetch tools; if it returns no usable text, the user sees a clear "not documented yet" message. See fallback behavior.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
First request

With the default route running:

`{ "question": "How do I install this?" }`

Use useOpenDetail for the Fastest path, or build your own client against the stream contract for Branded and Headless setups. See React.
chunks.title: Quickstart

chunks.text:
Use opendetail-react when you want OpenDetail in a React app. It gives you the browser state, the assistant components, and the wiring needed for the Fastest path. It also works well for the Branded path when you want your own layout but still want OpenDetail to manage retrieval and streaming.

If you are building a non-React client, use the transport client from opendetail-client or the Node engine in opendetail.
chunks.title: React

chunks.text:
What this package is for

Fastest: use the ready-made assistant components and keep your effort low.Branded: use the hook and primitives, then place them inside your own shell.Not this package: if you only want the transport client with no React state, use opendetail-client.
chunks.title: React

chunks.text:
The custom UI contract

OpenDetail keeps the frontend contract intentionally small.

Your UI sends:

{ "question": "..." }

The backend responds as a stream of NDJSON events. The important event types are meta, sources, images, delta, done, and error.

The order matters: metadata can arrive first, sources and images can appear as the assistant grounds itself, delta carries streamed text, and done or error ends the turn. If the request cannot start, the backend returns JSON instead of NDJSON.

That is the main thing a custom UI needs to know. Everything else is presentation.
chunks.title: React
React

chunks.text:
Assistant components

The package also ships higher-level React pieces for the Fastest path:

AssistantSidebar for a drop-in assistant panelAssistantModal for modal presentationThread, Composer, UserMessage, AssistantMessage, AssistantSources, and AssistantStatus for smaller custom compositions

These pieces are convenience layers on top of the same backend contract. If you outgrow them, you can keep the contract and replace the UI.
chunks.title: React

chunks.text:
Source links

For docs sites, renderNextSourceLink from opendetail-next turns local citations into safe Next.js links and keeps remote URLs as regular anchors. If your docs site already has predictable page URLs, pair that with opendetail-fumadocs so citation targets stay on known pages.
chunks.title: React

chunks.text:
Styling

opendetail-react includes a default visual system. Use it as-is for Fastest, or treat it as a base layer for Branded if you want to change the look without changing the contract.

The important separation is this:

changing tokens keeps you in the same systemchanging structure means you are really building a custom UI

That distinction helps keep the product easier to reason about for both adopters and maintainers.
chunks.title: React

chunks.text: Summaries align with the public changelog page. For full layout and dates, see the site changelog.
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.8.0 — Path-based setup and cleaner package boundaries
chunks.text: v0.8.0 — Path-based setup and cleaner package boundaries
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.8.0 — Path-based setup and cleaner package boundaries
chunks.text:
Setup

OpenDetail now explains adoption through Fastest, Branded, and Headless. The CLI matches that model with mode-first setup, a semantic doctor, and a new verify command for structural checks and optional live NDJSON validation.
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.8.0 — Path-based setup and cleaner package boundaries
chunks.text:
Packages

The NDJSON transport now lives in opendetail-client. opendetail-react is React-only, and opendetail-next stays focused on the Next.js route adapter and link helpers.
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.8.0 — Path-based setup and cleaner package boundaries
chunks.text:
Hardening

Public surfaces are tighter too: safer source-link handling, interrupted responses that no longer look complete, no third-party favicon lookups in the default sources UI, and an early request-size guard in the Next.js handler.

Changes: Added opendetail-client; rewrote docs around the adoption-path model; tightened link safety and privacy defaults in the React and Next integrations.
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.5.0 — Docs index and streaming UX
chunks.text: v0.5.0 — Docs index and streaming UX
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.5.0 — Docs index and streaming UX
chunks.text:
Build output

The CLI writes a single machine-readable index.json under .opendetail, so apps can load chunks for lexical search without a separate database. Paths stay stable across builds when content hashes do not change.
chunks.title: Changelog

headings: v0.5.0 — Docs index and streaming UX
chunks.text:
Assistant

The sidebar assistant accepts streamed NDJSON (text, sources, images) so the UI can render incrementally. Known doc URLs are passed through for safe source links in Fumadocs-style layouts.

Changes: Stricter validation for remote tool definitions; clearer errors when the index file is missing or out of date; minor CSS fixes for the input surface on small viewports.
chunks.title: Changelog

chunks.text:
v0.4.0 — CLI quickstart and configuration

opendetail setup scaffolds config, route, and initial index, with flags for base path, include globs, and optional media. opendetail doctor checks binary, config, and route wiring before you ship.

Changes: Configuration fields documented; improved progress output during opendetail build.
chunks.title: Changelog

chunks.text: OpenDetail — grounded answers from your own Markdown and MDX. Built for product teams who already ship docs and for developers who want a small, inspectable pipeline (local index file, narrow API, streaming events you can actually render).
chunks.title: OpenDetail

chunks.text:
In one sentence

Index docs at build time, answer from that index at request time, cite sources instead of guessing — same content humans read on the web, wired for your app and for agents that consume machine-readable routes.
chunks.title: OpenDetail

chunks.text:
Try the assistant here

Press ⌘ / Ctrl + J to open the sidebar and ask questions against this site's docs index.
chunks.title: OpenDetail
